intro:
The trial of Jesus proceeded through multiple stages spanning Jewish religious and Roman civil jurisdictions over a single night and morning. After his arrest in Gethsemane, Jesus was taken first to Annas, the former high priest and father-in-law of the current high priest Caiaphas (John 18:12-14, 19-23). He was then brought to Caiaphas and the Sanhedrin, where testimony was sought to justify a capital charge (Matt. 26:57-68; Mark 14:53-65). False witnesses offered inconsistent testimony about destroying the temple. Finally the high priest asked directly: 'Are you the Messiah, the Son of the Blessed One?' Jesus's affirmative answer — with explicit reference to Daniel 7:13 — was declared blasphemy, and the Sanhedrin voted for death. A morning session may have represented a formal ratification of the night's verdict (Luke 22:66-71).

Since the Sanhedrin lacked authority under Roman occupation to execute (John 18:31), Jesus was brought to the Roman governor Pontius Pilate. Pilate found no basis for the charge (Luke 23:4), sent him to Herod Antipas (Luke 23:6-12), and on his return offered to release him under the Passover amnesty — but the crowd demanded Barabbas. Pilate's declaration 'I find no basis for a charge against this man' (John 18:38; 19:4, 6) and his ultimate capitulation to crowd pressure fearing accusation before Caesar (John 19:12-16) are consistent with Roman provincial judicial practice under political pressure. The charge affixed to the cross — 'King of the Jews' (Matt. 27:37) — was the Roman charge of sedition. Early Christian interpretation saw both Jewish and Roman complicity in the trial as fulfilling prophetic patterns while noting that the primary initiative lay with the Jerusalem religious leadership (Acts 2:23; 4:27-28).
